lesson: A level with clean platforming but zero enemies or hazards can still feel flat and low-fun even with a perfect, fast clear — traversal alone isn't enough stimulation.
Fast completion time with zero deaths/falls signals the level was too easy/under-stimulating rather than well-tuned.
Enemies and hazard density can make a level feel fun and engaging even when the layout has no vertical variation — fun and 'verticality' are judged as separate axes by players.

Horizontal floor gaps/pits are not perceived as verticality by players even if platforms sit at different heights — true verticality needs traversal that changes the player's elevation (climbing/descending), not just gaps at one baseline.
Flying enemies placed above the player's maximum projectile arc create unfair, uncounterable threats that frustrate rather than challenge.
An exit placed on a high platform without climbable paths or vertical traversal options renders the goal unreachable, turning exploration into guesswork.
Flat levels with no vertical traversal (climbing/descending) and poorly positioned aerial enemies fail to deliver engaging platforming or combat.
A level that can be beaten by holding one direction with zero player inputs beyond movement provides no engagement and will always rate poorly regardless of polish.
Exit placement at the same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles turns the level into a walk simulator rather than a platformer.

Enemies that turrets or stationary shooters placed on wide horizontal platforms become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — elevation or vertical offset is required for threat relevance.
Repetitive enemy patterns without variation in positioning or timing create predictable, low-engagement encounters even with high enemy counts.
Turrets or stationary shooters placed on wide horizontal platforms become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — elevation or vertical offset is required for threat relevance.

Stationary shooters placed on the same elevation as the player's movement path become irrelevant if the player can pass beneath their firing line — vertical offset or elevation change is required to make them threatening.
Repeating identical platform-and-turret patterns without variation in spacing, height, or timing create predictable, low-engagement encounters that players optimize away.
A single repeated macro-pattern (e.g., stair-step hills) across an entire level creates visual and mechanical fatigue even if local geometry varies slightly.
Collision transitions between sloped ramps and flat ceilings produce unexpected falls that feel unfair rather than challenging.
Enemy variety without terrain variety still yields low engagement — enemies need diverse spatial contexts (verticality, cover, chokepoints) to create distinct encounters.
A level composed of a single repeated macro-structure (e.g., one continuous slope) creates mechanical fatigue even if local geometry varies slightly.
Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient.
Spawn points must be validated for clear vertical and horizontal clearance — at minimum one empty tile above and to each side — before a level is considered playable.
A level that prevents the first player input (movement/jump) fails before any design intent can be evaluated; structural validity is a prerequisite for fun, not a result of it.
